coredata.dc:title: An Ultrasensitive Turn-On Ratiometric Fluorescent Probes for Detection of Ag+ Based On Carbon Dots/SiO2 and Gold Nanoclusters
coredata.dc:creator: An, Jia
Chen, Rubing
Chen, Meizhu
Hu, Yongqin
Lyu, Ying
Liu, Yufei
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, ratiometric fluorescent probes have been widely used in biochemical sensors and bioimaging due to their accuracy, sensitivity, and visibility. In this paper, we constructed “turn-on” ratiometric fluorescent probes for the detection of Ag+ by coupling carbon dots-silicon spheres composites (named CSs) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs). The dual-emission fluorescent probes (CSA) exhibited two fluorescence emissions at 448 and 610 nm respectively, under a single excitation of 380 nm. When Ag+ was added, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs was enhanced thus, quantitative detection could be carried out within the ranges of 0-0.1 μM and 0.1-10 μM. The detection limit of Ag+ was 1.6 nM. Fascinatingly, when the concentration of Ag+ was increased, the fluorescence colour of the CSA ratiometric probes changed from blue to purple and then to pink. More importantly, the CSA composites were utilized to prepare portable agarose hydrogels for the detection of Ag+ by visualization. To the best of our knowledege, this is the first time ratiometric probes sensors have been used to visually detect Ag+. Therefore, this dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probes have the potential to be applied to the detection of Ag+ in environmental monitoring and food safety assessment. In particularly, this technology can also be extended to other analytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
gold nanoclusters
ratiometric fluorescent probes
silver ion
fluorescent microspheres

CSA composites agarose hydrogel test kits

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
National Key Research and Development Program of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of a) CSs composites (the inset graph is the corresponding size distribution), b) AuNCs (the inset graph is the corresponding size distribution), c) CSA composites material, d) HAADF–STEM image of the CSA composites material.

a) FTIR spectrum of AuNCs, CSs and CSA composites; b) XRD patterns of AuNCs, CSs and CSA composites; c) XPS patterns of AuNCs, CSs and CSA composites; d) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s of CSA composites.

a) Fluorescent emission spectra of AuNCs, CSs and CSA composites (the inset is the photos of AuNCs, CSs and CSA composites under UV-light); b) Fluorescent emission spectra of CSA composites under different excitation wavelengths (the inset is the drawing of partial enlargement).

The fluorescence intensity of the CSs composite towards the effects of a) storage days (the inset is the fluorescence spectrum of storage days); b) pH; c) NaCl concentration; d) UV-light illumination time.

a) Fluorescent emission spectra of CSA composites with different concentrations of Ag+ (the inset is the drawing of partial enlargement); b) Relationship between I628/I448 and concentration of Ag+ (the inset is the drawing of partial enlargement).
a) Specificity and selectivity of the CSA composites towards Ag+; b) Specificity and selectivity of the CSA composites towards Ag+ under UV-light.
Fluorescent colours of CSA composites hydrogels change with different concentration of Ag+ under UV-light.
Processing diagram for the synthesis of CSA composites and Ag+ detection.
Detection of Ag+ in real samples.

head.ce:title: An Ultrasensitive Turn-On Ratiometric Fluorescent Probes for Detection of Ag+ Based On Carbon Dots/SiO2 and Gold Nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems (Chongqing University), Ministry of Education, Chongqing 400044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems (Chongqing University)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems (Chongqing University), Ministry of Education, Chongqing 400044, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems (Chongqing University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, ratiometric fluorescent probes have been widely used in biochemical sensors and bioimaging due to their accuracy, sensitivity, and visibility. In this paper, we constructed “turn-on” ratiometric fluorescent probes for the detection of Ag+ by coupling carbon dots-silicon spheres composites (named CSs) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs). The dual-emission fluorescent probes (CSA) exhibited two fluorescence emissions at 448 and 610 nm respectively, under a single excitation of 380 nm. When Ag+ was added, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs was enhanced thus, quantitative detection could be carried out within the ranges of 0-0.1 μM and 0.1-10 μM. The detection limit of Ag+ was 1.6 nM. Fascinatingly, when the concentration of Ag+ was increased, the fluorescence colour of the CSA ratiometric probes changed from blue to purple and then to pink. More importantly, the CSA composites were utilized to prepare portable agarose hydrogels for the detection of Ag+ by visualization. To the best of our knowledege, this is the first time ratiometric probes sensors have been used to visually detect Ag+. Therefore, this dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probes have the potential to be applied to the detection of Ag+ in environmental monitoring and food safety assessment. In particularly, this technology can also be extended to other analytes.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs), as one of the most fascinating fluorescent materials among zero-dimensional materials discovered in 2004[1], have drawn worldwide attention due to their excellent photostability, good biocompatibility, tuneable fluorescence emission and other advantages[2–6]. The methods of preparing CDs developed so far include the hydrothermal method, chemical oxidation method, electrochemical oxidation method, laser ablation method, and so on[7–11]. Furthermore, the applications of CDs primarily focus on detection, imaging, photocatalysis, photoelectric devices, and other fields[12–15]. From some papers, we found that most synthesis methods of the CDs are simple, but the purification methods are more complicated and costly, which is not conducive to large-scale production. Moreover, the prepared CDs could not have specificity in the detection field, and were easily affected by many interfering substances for application in the real environment. Therefore, many researchers have used silicon (SiO2) sphere protection to improve the stability of CDs and simplify the purification process[16–18]. Wang et al[19]. used a one-pot hydrothermal method to load CDs on the surface of SiO2 spheres, which could greatly improve the purification efficiency of samples and maintain a high quantum yield. The authors utilized this material for bioimaging, providing an effective platform for cancer diagnosis. Zhao et al[20]. used silicon functionalized CDs and TEOS as precursors to synthesize CDs-SiO2 composites and then successfully applied them in fingerprint detection. This material had high sensitivity and selectivity and showed obvious advantages compared with traditional materials. Rao et al[21]. used SiO2 spheres coated with CDs and covalently coupled with cadmium-telluride quantum dots to form a ratiometric probe to detect copper ions. CDs coated with SiO2 spheres had high stability and would not be quenched by copper ions, thus achieving the purpose of detection.

Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), as emerging fluorescent nanomaterials, have excellent advantages such as great optical stability, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity, and have great application potential in the field of analytical chemistry [22–25]. The conventional methods of preparing AuNCs are divided into the top-down method and the bottom-up method [26–29]. Moreover, AuNCs synthesized by different materials have different fluorescence emissions and different functional groups, which have the potential for further modification and application. Therefore, these advantages of AuNCs have gradually attracted attention in the fields of catalysis, bioimaging, biochemical sensors, and cancer diagnosis and treatment [30–32].

As we all know, traces of silver ions (Ag+) can be seen everywhere in our daily life, especially in the fields of photography, electronics, and imaging [33,34]. However, Ag+ is also a common heavy metal ion, which greatly endangers our environment and personal safety. Excessive consumption of Ag+ has been reported to cause silver poisoning, slow growth, skin damage, and other diseases [35,36]. Therefore, highly sensitive detection of Ag+ is particularly important. At present, the main detection methods include the atomic absorption spectrum, electrochemical method, fluorescence method, and so on [37,38]. As one of the newest fluorescence detection methods, ratiometric fluorescent probes have attracted increasing attention in recent years, because they have significant advantages over a single fluorescent probe. The ratiometric fluorescent probes are dependent on analyte-induced changes in the fluorescence intensity (FL) of two or more. These probes have an effective internal reference system, which can eliminate the influence of various irrelevant factors in the environment (instrument efficiency, probe concentration, environmental interference, etc.) and greatly improve the sensitivity of the probe [39–42].

Herein, we fabricated carbon dots-silicon sphere (CSs) composites by in-situ growth of CDs on SiO2 spheres using a hydrothermal method. Glutathione stable AuNCs were coupled to the surface of the CSs composites via covalent bonds, CSs-AuNCs (CSA) ratiometric fluorescent probes were successfully prepared with emissions peaking at 448 nm and 610 nm respectively, under single excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Different from the blue fluorescence of CSs composites and the orange fluorescence of AuNCs, the ratiometric fluorescent probes show purple fluorescence under UV light (λex = 360 nm). The construction of CSA ratiometric fluorescent probes and the principle of Ag+ detection are shown in Scheme1 . The addition of Ag+ to the system results in the fluorescence increased of AuNCs, while the fluorescence of the CSs composites was unchanged. Therefore, the ratio of the two fluorescence intensities in the CSA ratiometric fluorescent probes will be changed to achieve the purpose of the quantitative detection of Ag+. This work fabricated a fast, accurate, high sensitivity and selectivity sensor for Ag+ detection.

5 mL of 0.55 M CA solution, 5 mL of SiO2 spheres (60 nm, 30 mg/mL) and 335 μL of 15 M EDA were added to a Teflon-lined autoclave. After the solution was evenly mixed, the reaction lasted for 5 h at 200 °C.
After the reaction, the sample was washed several times with ethanol and water until the supernatant had no fluorescence.

40 mL of ultrapure water were added to the round-bottomed flask, and then 4 mL of HAuCl4 solution (25 mM) and 6 mL of GSH solution (25 mM) were added. The samples were stirred vigorously at room temperature until colourless, and then gently stirred at 70 °C for 24 h. The samples were dialyzed for 3 days. Furthermore, an ultrafiltration tube was used for ultrafiltration, then the samples with 3-10 kDa were collected for further study.

Then, 4 mL of AuNCs sample was added to 40 Ml of 10% APTES and stirred for 1 h, and then 2 mL CSs (100 mg/mL) sample was added and stirred for 4 h. Then the sample was washed several times with ethanol and water until the supernatant had no fluorescence.

The CSA nanohybrid was dissolved in ultrapure water containing various concentrations of Ag+. The final concentrations of Ag+ presented were 0.003, 0.01, 0.025, 0.04, 0.05, 0.1, 1, 5, 8, 10 and 50 μM. The solution was mixed thoroughly at room temperature. Fluorescence spectra were then obtained in the wavelength range of 400–730 nm with excitation at 380 nm. The selectivity of this sensing system for Ag+ was assessed using K+, Na+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Ba2+ and Ca2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of portable CSA composites agarose hydrogel test kits
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 0.05 g agarose was added to 5 mL CSA composites solution and heated until the agarose was completely dissolved. A 600 μL solution was removed and added to the templates. The CSA composites agarose hydrogel was formed in a few minutes at room temperature.

Yangtze River samples were collected to test the recovery. The Yangtze River was filtered and diluted 5 times with ultrapure water, and Ag+ of different concentrations was added to prepare the spiked samples.

For the fabrication of the CSA composites, SiO2 nanospheres with a particle size of approximately 57 nm (Fig. S1) were prepared by the Stöber method. TEM clearly showed that SiO2 nanospheres had uniform particle size and good dispersion. Fluorescent CDs loaded on SiO2 nanospheres are synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal process. Fig. 1 a shows that the prepared CSs composites have almost identical morphology and particle size as SiO2 nanospheres, indicating that the process of in-situ growth of CDs does not influence the morphology and size of SiO2 nanospheres. By comparing the TEM of CSs composites and SiO2 nanospheres, it is found that the surface of CSs composites has some small white dots, and the morphology was strawberry-like. However, the surface of the SiO2 nanospheres (Fig. S1) was very smooth and flat, confirming that the CDs in the composite grew on the surface of the SiO2 nanospheres. The TEM of the GSH-stabilized AuNCs (Fig. 1b) shows that the particle size was approximately 1.7 nm, with uniform particle size and good dispersion. AuNCs were functionalized by APTES to covalently bind to the surface of the CSs composites, constituting CSA composites material. AuNCs on the surface of CSs composites can be proven by Fig. 1c-d.

Fig. 2 a shows the FT-IR spectrum of the three materials (AuNCs, CSs, CSA). The characteristic peaks of 1540 and 1652 cm-1 of AuNCs were assigned C = O stretching vibrations, suggesting that the prepared AuNCs have carboxyl groups [31]. The FT-IR spectra of CSA and CSs were similar, revealing that conjugated AuNCs did not affect the surface structure of the CSA composites [43]. Additionally, the XRD pattern (Fig. 2b) of CSA composites is consistent with the planes of CSs composites and AuNCs. This result also suggested the successful preparation of CSA composites. Fig. 2c displays the XPS survey spectrum of AuNCs, CSs and CSA composites, which shows that CSA composites consisted of characteristic peaks of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Si 2s, Si 2p and Au 4f, indicating that AuNCs were successfully coupled to the CSA composites. The high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 2d) of the C 1s band exhibited three peaks at 284.5, 285.8 and 287.5 eV, which are assigned to C-C/C=C, C-N, and C-OH bonds [44,45]. N 1s (Fig. S2a), O 1s (Fig. S 2b) and Si 2p (Fig. S2c) analysis revealed that CSA composites contain N–C, N–H, C–O, C=O, Si-O-C, and Si-Ox bonds [46]. The high-resolution spectrum (Fig. S2d) of Au 4f exhibited two peaks at 87.8 and 84.1 eV, which is consistent with the result of AuNCs (Fig. S3). The XPS results also demonstrated the successful preparation of CSA composites.

The fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3 a) exhibit strong fluorescence emission of single CSs composites and AuNCs at 445 nm and 610 nm, respectively (λex = 380 nm). While CSA composites simultaneously displayed two maximal emissions at 448 nm and 610 nm under a single excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The characteristic peak of CSs had a slight shift, suggesting that CSs and AuNCs were covalently coupled rather than physically mixed. The photographs of CSs, AuNCs and CSA under UV-light exhibited blue, orange and purple fluorescence, respectively (inset in Fig. 3a). Therefore, the successful synthesis of CSA composites was evidenced by the above characterization methods. Moreover, combining the excitation (Fig. S4) and emission spectra (Fig. 3b) of CSA based on the integrality and intensity relationship of two characteristic peaks, confirming that the optimum excitation wavelength is 380 nm.

As everyone knows, the photoluminescence (PL) stability of fluorescent materials is very important for their applications. More particularly, the material involved in the ratiometric fluorescent probes was used as a reference signal. Thus, to verify the accuracy of the ratiometric fluorescent probes, we examined the PL of the CSs composites under different conditions. Fig. 4 a displays the fluorescence intensity of the CSs composites solution after storage for 64 days. This indicated that even without extra protection, the fluorescence intensity of the CSs composites could remain unchanged for a long time. Furthermore, when the CSs composites was dispersed in Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solutions with different pH values (Fig. 4b), it was also clear that there was almost no significant change in fluorescence intensity, indicating that the CSs composite had a very high tolerance to acid/alkali aqueous solution. Additionally, the change in fluorescence intensity can be ignored when the CSs composite is dispersed in different concentrations of NaCl solution (Fig. 4c). Surprisingly, even if the CSs composite was continuously irradiated by UV-light for 10 min, the fluorescence intensity remained approximately constant, which presented a better bleaching resistance (Fig. 4d). These results proved that the CSs composite have excellent PL stability, making them promising for biological applications with reliability and practicability.

Surprisingly, the investigated results found (Fig. S5) that when different metal ions were added to the AuNCs solution, only Ag+ significantly enhanced the fluorescence of the AuNCs. The metal sensitivity of CSs composites only shows the fluorescence quenching of Fe3+ (Fig. S6). Therefore, CSA composite probes was established as a fluorescence “turn-on” strategy for the detection of Ag+. From a previous paper, it could be seen that the fluorescence enhancement mechanism of Ag+ on AuNCs is mainly due to the change of charge transfer in the shell caused by the immobilization of Ag+ on the surface electron-rich groups, which affects the change of FL of AuNCs[47,48]. To confirm this result, we investigated the zeta potentials of the CSA composite probes before and after the addition of Ag+, and found that the zeta potentials were -29.49 mV and -10.30 mV in the absence and presence of Ag+ (Fig. S7), which is consistent with the above explanation. In order to evaluate the sensitivity of AuNCs to Ag+ detection, different concentrations of Ag+ and AuNCs were incubated under optimal conditions. As seen from the fluorescence spectrum in Fig. S8, along with the concentration of Ag+ increased, following the FL of AuNCs at 610 nm will also increase. Meanwhile, when the concentration of Ag+ was too large, the emission peak had a small shift. It shows a good linear relationship in the range of 0-0.5 μM, and the detection limit of Ag+ was 5 nM according to the formula (LOD = 3 N/S, N is the standard deviation of blank data, S is the slope of the calibration line). This result indicated that we could utilize CSA ratiometric fluorescent probes to quantitatively detect Ag+.

To confirm the optimal conditions for CSA ratiometric fluorescent probes to detect Ag+, we determined the optimal pH conditions, buffer and reaction time for the detection. As shown in Fig. S9, the FL variation trend of the CSA composites at different pH values was first enhanced and then weakened with the strongest FL at pH 7. Moreover, in order to further verify the optimal buffer solution, phosphate buffer (pH = 6.8 and pH = 7.2), Britton-Robinson buffer (pH = 7.0) and HEPES buffer (pH = 7.4) were selected and tested under the same conditions. As the Fig. S10 exhibits, phosphate buffer pH = 7.2 (PB 7.2) was found to be the best. Therefore, all experiments in this work were performed in PB 7.2 solution. Simultaneously, Fig. S11 shows that when the reaction time reached 10 min, the FL no longer changed. Thus, the optimal reaction time was 10 min. As shown in Fig. 5 a-b, the FL of AuNCs increased markedly with increasing of Ag+ concentration, while the FL of CSs composites remained unchanged. At the same time, the emission peak produced a slight red-shift from 610 nm to 628 nm. When the concentration of Ag+ was in the range 0-0.1 μM and 0.1-10 μM, the FL ratio I628/I448 and the Ag+ concentration had a good linear relationship, and the linear regression equation is Y = 0.46 [CAg +] +0.12 (0-0.1 μM, R2 = 0.997) and Y = 0.007 [CAg +] +0.16 (0.1-10 μM, R2 = 0.991). Therefore, the detection limit (LOD = 3 N/S, N is the standard deviation of blank data, S is the slope of the calibration line) of the sensing system for Ag+ was 1.6 nM. The results demonstrated that the CSA ratiometric fluorescent probes has better performance than single AuNCs probes. Furthermore, as Table S1 reveals, compared with other analytical methods for detecting Ag+, this dual-emission fluorescent probes has obvious advantages in detection sensitivity. Moreover, ratiometric fluorescence probes have excellent accuracy compared with single fluorescent probes, which can effectively eliminate background signals and environmental interference, resulting in high sensitivity and selectivity.

In order to verify the feasibility of this CSA ratiometric fluorescent probes in the detection of Ag+ in complex environments, we collected some potential metal ion interferences (Cu2+, Na+, K+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, and Mg2+) to verify the specificity of the CSA ratiometric fluorescent probes in detecting Ag+. As shown in Fig. 6 a-b, when Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+ and Ni2+ were added to the system, the fluorescence of AuNCs could be quenched. As a result, the ratio of FL would eventually be reduced, showing blue fluorescence under the UV-light. However, when Na+, K+, Ba2+, Ca2+ and Mg2+ were added to the system, the FL ratio remained unchanged and showed purple fluorescence under the UV-light, since these metal ions did not affect the fluorescence of AuNCs and CSs composites. However, when we added Ag+, which could enhance the fluorescence of AuNCs, the ratio of FL increased and showed fuchsia fluorescence under the UV-light. Therefore, it is proven that the selectivity and specificity of CSA ratiometric fluorescent probes for Ag+ detection was higher than that of other metal ions and could achieve the capability of naked-eye detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CSA composites agarose hydrogel test kits
ce:section.ce:para: As is known to all, it is of great practical significance to be able to detect Ag+ on site and in situ with the naked eye. Therefore, the CSA composites were utilized to prepare portable agarose hydrogels for the visualization detection of Ag+. Fig. 7 displays photographs of CSA composites hydrogels with different concentrations of Ag+ in white and UV-light. It can be seen that as the concentration of Ag+ increased, the colour of the hydrogel changed from blue to purple and then to pink under the UV-light. Within a range of 0.001-1000 mM, the concentration of Ag+ can be easily distinguished with the naked eye. Therefore, the CSA composites hydrogels have great potential and prospects for semi-quantitative analysis of Ag+ with the naked eye.

Furthermore, in order to evaluate the application of the CSA ratiometric fluorescent probes in the real samples, the concentration of Ag+ in river water was detected by the standard addition method. The collected river water from the Yangtze River was filtered and diluted 5 times with ultrapure water. From Table 1 , it could know that the recovery rate of the samples was in the range 98-105%, RSD ≤ 4.46. Compared with the results of some standard methods (Table. S2), our method shows excellent reliability and accuracy. More importantly, the constructed detection method has the advantages of easy operation, rapid response and visualization, indicating that this method has great reliability and potential in testing Ag+ in real samples.

In conclusion, a turn-on ratiometric fluorescent probes platform for Ag+ detection was constructed by coupling CSs composites with AuNCs via covalent bonding. The CSA ratiometric fluorescent probes exhibited two different fluorescence emission (445 nm and 610 nm) simultaneously under a single excitation, and the FL of AuNCs was significantly enhanced when Ag+ was added, while the FL of CSs remained unchanged. When the Ag+ concentration was within the range 0-0.1 μM and 0.1-10 μM, the ratio of FL to the Ag+ concentration showed a good linear relationship, and the detection limit was as low as 1.6 nM. At the same time, the experimental results show that the CSA ratiometric fluorescent probes has high selectivity in detecting Ag+, and could also identify Ag+ with the naked eye. This proved that CSA ratiometric fluorescent probes have great potential for detection in the real environment and great demonstration significance for the detection of heavy metal ions.

Jia An: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Rubing Chen: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Meizhu Chen: Investigation, Resources. Yongqin Hu: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Ying Lyu: Investigation, Resources. Yufei Liu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Chongqing Entrepreneurship and Innovation Support Program (CX201803), China Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities(Grant No. 2019CDQYGD020, 2019CDCGGD304 and 2020CDJGFCG006), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2016YFC0101100 and 2016YFE0125200). Thanks for the test platform provided by the Analytical and Testing Center of Chongqing University and and Beijing Zhongkebaice Technology Service Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.129097.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: The following is supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Yao, H. Huang, Y. Liu, Z. Kang, Carbon Dots: A Small Conundrum, Trends in Chemistry, 1(2019), pp. 235-46.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An aqueous route to multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots using silica spheres as carriers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Liu, D. Wu, S. Liu, K. Koynov, W. Knoll, Q. Li, An aqueous route to multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots using silica spheres as carriers, Angewandte Chemie, 48(2009), pp. 4598-601.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon Dots as Nontoxic and High-Performance Fluorescence Imaging Agents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: h.-T. Yang, X. Wang, H. Wang, F. Lu, Carbon Dots as Nontoxic and High-Performance Fluorescence Imaging Agents, J Phys Chem C 113(2009), pp. 18110–4.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Journal of the American Chemical Society

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Cao, X. Wang, M.J. Meziani, Carbon Dots for Multiphoton Bioimaging, Journal of the American Chemical Society, 129(2007), pp. 11318-9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanodots as ligand exchange probes in Au@C-dot nanobeacons for fluorescent turn-on detection of biothiols

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Mandani, B. Sharma, D. Dey, T.K. Sarma, Carbon nanodots as ligand exchange probes in Au@C-dot nanobeacons for fluorescent turn-on detection of biothiols, Nanoscale, 7(2015), pp. 1802-8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple Aqueous Solution Route to Luminescent Carbogenic Dots from Carbohydrates

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Peng, J. Travas-Sejdic, Simple Aqueous Solution Route to Luminescent Carbogenic Dots from Carbohydrates, Chemistry of Materials, 21(2009), pp. 5563-5.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties, and sensing applications as a potential clinical analytical method

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Huang, W. Li, P. Han, X. Zhou, J. Cheng, H. Wen, et al., Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties, and sensing applications as a potential clinical analytical method, Analytical Methods, 11(2019), pp. 2240-58.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile preparation of low cytotoxicity fluorescent carbon nanocrystals by electrooxidation of graphite

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q.L. Zhao, Z.L. Zhang, B.H. Huang, J. Peng, M. Zhang, D.W. Pang, Facile preparation of low cytotoxicity fluorescent carbon nanocrystals by electrooxidation of graphite, Chemical communications, (2008), pp. 5116-8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Laser Ablated Carbon Nanodots for Light Emission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Reyes, M. Camacho, M. Camacho, M. Mayorga, D. Weathers, G. Salamo, et al., Laser Ablated Carbon Nanodots for Light Emission, Nanoscale research letters, 11(2016), p. 424.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence-tunable carbon nanodots: surface-state energy-gap tuning

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Bao, C. Liu, Z.L. Zhang, D.W. Pang, Photoluminescence-tunable carbon nanodots: surface-state energy-gap tuning, Advanced materials, 27(2015), pp. 1663-7.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, S. Sun, L. Zhang, Y. Lu, A. Wu, C. Cai, et al., Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging, Angewandte Chemie, 54(2015), pp. 5360-3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ammonium hydroxide modulated synthesis of high-quality fluorescent carbon dots for white LEDs with excellent color rendering properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Wang, Z. Zhu, Y. Chang, H. Wang, N. Yuan, G. Li, et al., Ammonium hydroxide modulated synthesis of high-quality fluorescent carbon dots for white LEDs with excellent color rendering properties, Nanotechnology, 27(2016), p. 295202.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dot supported semiconductor photocatalysts for efficient degradation of organic pollutants in water: A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Sharma, V. Dutta, P. Singh, P. Raizada, A. Rahmani-Sani, A. Hosseini-Bandegharaei, et al., Carbon quantum dot supported semiconductor photocatalysts for efficient degradation of organic pollutants in water: A review, Journal of Cleaner Production, 228(2019), pp. 755-69.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile access to versatile fluorescent carbon dots toward light-emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Guo, C.F. Wang, Z.Y. Yu, L. Chen, S. Chen, Facile access to versatile fluorescent carbon dots toward light-emitting diodes, Chemical communications, 48(2012), pp. 2692-4.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-color carbon dots with multiple red-emission tuning: on/off sensors, in vitro and in vivo multicolor bioimaging

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver ions enhanced AuNCs fluorescence as a turn-off nanoprobe for ultrasensitive detection of iodide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Hou, Y. Chen, Q. Lu, M. Liu, Y. Zhang, S. Yao, Silver ions enhanced AuNCs fluorescence as a turn-off nanoprobe for ultrasensitive detection of iodide, Talanta, 180(2018), pp. 144-9

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Miss Jia An has been awarded the B.S. in Pharmaceutical engineering and the MSc. in chemical engineering and technology from Yichun University and Taiyuan University in 2014 and 2017, respectively. Now is a Ph.D. candidate at College of OptoEngineering, Chongqing University (400044 China).
Miss Rubing Chen has been awarded the bachelor's degree from Chongqing University in 2018. Now is a master student at College of OptoEngineering, Chongqing University (400044 China).
Miss Meizhu Chen has been awarded the bachelor's degree from Chongqing University in 2018. Now is a master candidate at College of OptoEngineering, Chongqing University (400044 China).

Miss Yongqin Hu has been awarded the bachelor's degree and the master’s degree from Zunyi Teachers College and China West Normal University in 2013 and 2016, respectively. Now is a Ph.D. candidate at College of OptoEngineering, Chongqing University (400044 China).
Miss Ying Lyu has been awarded the bachelor's degree from Harbin Institute of Technology in 2018. Now is a master candidate at College of OptoEngineering, Chongqing University (400044 China).

Dr. Yufei Liu curretly is a Professor in Micro and Nano Technology, College of OptoEngineering, Chongqing University (400044 China) and the Honorary Research Fellows in Centre for NanoHealth, Swansea University (SA2 8 P P UK). Dr. Liu has been awarded the B.S. in Physic, MEng in microelectronics and solid state electronics and Ph.D. in Electronic Engineering from Peking University, Chinese Academy of Science and Heriot-Watt University in 2003, 2006 and 2011, respectively. Dr. Liu was working in Swansea University and Imperial College London as a Research Fellow between Jan 2010 and June 2014.
